title: Conditional Statements (if / else if / else)
description: Making decisions in code with if, else if, and else, building on the boolean and relational/logical operators from Java Fundamentals.
objectives: Use if to run code only when a condition is true
Use else to run alternative code when the condition is false
Chain multiple conditions with else if
Trace which branch of an if/else if/else chain executes

sections.text: So far, every program we've written runs every line, top to bottom, no matter what. Real programs need to make decisions — like showing a discount only if a purchase is over a certain amount. In the Operators lesson, we built boolean expressions like age >= 18 without a way to act on them. Today we act on them.
A statement that runs a block of code only when a given boolean condition evaluates to true.
The condition (age >= 18) is a boolean expression, exactly like the ones from the Operators lesson. If it evaluates to true, the code inside { } runs. If it's false, that block is skipped entirely.

sections.title: Umbrella analogy
sections.text: "IF it's raining, take an umbrella." If it's not raining, you simply don't take one — nothing else happens. That's exactly how a plain if statement without else behaves.
else provides a block that runs only when the if condition was false. Exactly one of the two blocks runs — never both, never neither.
Sometimes there are more than two possible outcomes. else if lets you chain additional conditions.

Java checks conditions top to bottom and stops at the FIRST one that's true. Here, marks = 75 satisfies both marks >= 75 and marks >= 60, but only "Grade: B" prints, because Java never even looks at the later else if once an earlier branch matched.

sections.title: Tracing the grade example
points: marks >= 75? 75 >= 75 is true — run this block, print "Grade: B", skip everything else in the chain
The final else never runs, because an earlier branch already matched

sections.text: An if statement can contain another if statement inside its block — this is called nesting.

This nested version is logically equivalent to if (age >= 18 && hasId) using the && logical operator from the Operators lesson — but nesting can be clearer when each condition needs its own distinct else message, like here.
6. Curly Braces Are Optional for Single Statements

If an if or else has only ONE statement, the { } are technically optional. This is strongly discouraged — it's easy to accidentally think a second line is part of the if block when it isn't, causing confusing bugs. Always use { } in this course, even for one-line blocks.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using = instead of == inside a condition — if (age = 18) is an assignment, not a comparison, and generally won't even compile for a non-boolean variable in Java.
Ordering else if conditions so a broader one comes before a narrower one — e.g. checking marks >= 60 before marks >= 90 means the >= 90 branch can never be reached.
Assuming multiple else if blocks can all run — only the first one whose condition is true runs; the rest are skipped automatically.
Omitting { } on multi-line blocks by accident, causing only the first line to actually belong to the if.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the purpose of an if statement?
questions.answer: It runs a block of code only when a given boolean condition evaluates to true, allowing a program to make decisions instead of always running the same instructions.

questions.question: In an if / else if / else chain, how many blocks actually execute?
questions.answer: Exactly one — Java evaluates the conditions top to bottom and executes the block belonging to the first true condition, skipping the rest of the chain entirely, including the final else.

questions.question: Why does condition order matter in an else if chain?
questions.answer: Because Java stops at the first true condition. If a broader condition (like marks >= 60) is placed before a narrower one (like marks >= 90), the narrower branch may never be reached even when it should logically apply.

questions.question: What is a nested if statement?
questions.answer: An if statement placed inside the block of another if (or else) statement, used when a decision depends on more than one condition evaluated in sequence.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: if, else if, and else let a Java program make decisions instead of always executing the same code. An if runs its block only when its boolean condition is true; else provides a fallback block for when it's false; else if chains additional conditions, checked top to bottom, with only the first true branch's block executing. Conditions can be nested inside each other for multi-factor decisions, and curly braces should always be used even for single-statement blocks to avoid ambiguity.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: if/else if/else is a ladder — Java climbs down rung by rung and stops at the FIRST true rung. Once it stops, it never checks the rungs below.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: In an if/else if/else chain, how many blocks run at most?
Why does condition order matter in an else if chain?
What happens if you omit { } around a single-statement if block?
